title: Oxford Economics launches AskOE Executive Lens
gist: Oxford Economics launched a beta conversational AI tool, AskOEAI Executive Lens, that personalizes its proprietary economic research by user role, sector and geography, positioning it as a premium alternative to general-purpose AI tools reliant on the firm's four-decade research moat.

themes.theme: Research firms productizing proprietary data via AI

insights.claim: Oxford Economics is betting that its defensible moat against general-purpose AI (ChatGPT-style tools) is proprietary, exclusive data and forecasts rather than the AI layer itself, since Executive Lens draws exclusively on Oxford Economics' own analysis and forecasts rather than external or public data.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Watch for other research/advisory incumbents (e.g., Gartner, IHS Markit/S&P Global, Moody's Analytics) racing to wrap proprietary datasets in personalized AI agents to defend subscription revenue against generic LLM substitution.
insights.quote: Unlike general-purpose AI tools, Executive Lens is purpose-built by Oxford Economics’ economists and technology team and draws exclusively on Oxford Economics’ proprietary analysis and forecasts.
insights.locator: Unlike general-purpose AI tools, Executive Lens is purpose-built

insights.claim: Oxford Economics frames its AI strategy explicitly as augmenting rather than replacing human expert judgment, per CEO Innes McFee's statement that 'AI should make trusted expertise easier to access, not replace it.'
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Signals continued demand for human-curated research overlays even as AI adoption scales; suggests advisory firms see full automation of expert analysis as a reputational risk, not just a technical one.
insights.quote: AI should make trusted expertise easier to access, not replace it.
insights.locator: Since launching AskOEAI in September 2024, we have continued to invest

insights.claim: This is Oxford Economics' second AI platform milestone in under two years, following the original AskOEAI launch in September 2024, indicating a sustained, incremental product-investment cadence rather than a one-off AI initiative.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: A roughly 21-month cadence between major AI feature launches suggests Oxford Economics is treating AI as a core, recurring R&D line item, useful context for gauging spend intensity among mid-size research/advisory firms.

insights.claim: Oxford Economics describes itself as covering over 200 countries, 100 industrial sectors, and 8,000 cities and regions, underscoring the breadth of proprietary data it is now trying to monetize through the Executive Lens AI layer.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: The breadth of coverage (8,000 cities/regions) is the core asset being defended and repackaged via AI personalization; useful benchmark when comparing to rival macro research providers' data footprints.
insights.quote: Covering over 200 countries, 100 industrial sectors and 8,000 cities and regions

insights.claim: The related-content list on this release shows Oxford Economics has recently been active in inorganic growth, including acquiring Macromonitor (Australian construction economics) and taking a majority stake in Alpine Macro, alongside a GBP 2 million investment in The Data City for AI-powered business intelligence.
insights.speaker: Sally Li
insights.so_what: Oxford Economics is simultaneously expanding via bolt-on acquisitions and building AI distribution, suggesting a two-pronged strategy of buying niche data/research assets and layering a common AI interface on top to cross-sell them.
